coredata.dc:title: An innovative biodevice towards monitoring of miR-153 using specific DNA immobilized on the surface of poly(chitosan) decorated AgNPrs/GQDs-CysA conductive nano-ink: Early-stage diagnosis of Parkinson’s disease using biosensor technology
coredata.dc:creator: Darvish Aminabad, Esmaeil
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Saadati, Arezoo
Ali Hosseinpour Feizi, Mohammad
Safaralizadeh, Reza
Mobed, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Parkinson’s disease (PD), as the world’s second most common disabling age-related disease has caused motor and non-motor symptoms such as mental and behavioral changes, sleep problems, depression, memory difficulties. In recent years, several advantages of microRNAs (miRNAs), including resistance to RNAase digestion, stability, and extreme situations in circulating fluids, have led to received enormous attention for the detection of various diseases. miR-153 is one of the high potential related biomarkers of PDs which regulate α-Synuclein expression levels. Here, a sensitive and selective electrochemical biosensor was developed to recognize of miR-153 in human plasma samples. For the first time, the surface of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was decorated with poly-chitosan (CS) and silver nanoprisms/graphene quantum dots/ cysteamine A (AgNPrs/GQDs-Cys A) ink for the excellent immobilization of DNA towards sensitive and selective monitoring of micRNA-153. The designed novel electrochemical biosensor could detect miR-153 by differential pulse voltammetry with a linear range of 6.25 µM to 50 µM, which low limit of quantification is 6.25 µM. According to high selectivity, sensitivity, fast and easy operation this biodevice promising for clinical applications and high-throughput screening of PDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Parkinson
Electrochemical biosensor
miR-153
Nano-ink
Biological macromolecule
DNA
Biomedical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical evolution of biosensor fabricated steps
Analytical performance of developed biosensor in real sample
Long-term and short-term stability of fabricated ink and biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University of Tabriz and Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the University of Tabriz and Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A-I) FE-SEM images of GCE/poly-CS/ink/DNA in different magnification and J) EDX result of GCE/poly-CS/ink/DNA.
Photographic images of resistivity and conductivity assessment of hand-drawn paths by AgNPrs/GQDs-Cys A nano-ink on photographic paper substrate.

A) DPVs of various modified surfaces including GCE, GCE/P(CS)/, GCE/P(CS)/ink, GCE/P(CS)/ink/DNA, GCE/P(CS)/ink/DNA/MCH and GCE/P(CS)/ink/DNA/MCH/miRNA-153 in a potential range of −1 to + 1 V and scan rate of 100 mV/s in the solution of 0.01 Fe(CN6)3-/4/KCl. B) Histogram of peak current versus type of electrode.

A) DPVs of GCE/poly(CS)/ink/DNA in the presence of different concentration of miR-153 (50, 25, 12.5, and 6.25 µM) in a potential range of −1 V to + 1 V and scan rate of 100 mV/s and in the solution of 0.01 Fe(CN6)3-/4-/KCl B) Histogram of peak current versus various concentration of miR-153.

A) DPVs of the prepared biosensor for investigation of selectivity in the presence of different interferes on the modification electrode in the potential range of −1 to + 1 V and scan rate of 100 mV/s. B) Histogram of peak currents versus of interferes agents (miR-154 (sequence, UUG CAU AGU CAC AAA AGU GAUC), three mismatches (sequence, UAG CAU AGU CAG AAA AGU CAUC) and non-complement DNA with a sequence of GCC CGG AGA GCU GGG AGC CAG). Supporting electrolyte was 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−/KCl solution.
The illustration preparation of developed electrochemical biosensor for detection of miR-153.

head.ce:title: An innovative biodevice towards monitoring of miR-153 using specific DNA immobilized on the surface of poly(chitosan) decorated AgNPrs/GQDs-CysA conductive nano-ink: Early-stage diagnosis of Parkinson’s disease using biosensor technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran (M. Hasanzadeh), Department of Biology, Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran (M. Ali Hosseinpour Feizi).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Parkinson’s disease (PD), as the world’s second most common disabling age-related disease has caused motor and non-motor symptoms such as mental and behavioral changes, sleep problems, depression, memory difficulties. In recent years, several advantages of microRNAs (miRNAs), including resistance to RNAase digestion, stability, and extreme situations in circulating fluids, have led to received enormous attention for the detection of various diseases. miR-153 is one of the high potential related biomarkers of PDs which regulate α-Synuclein expression levels. Here, a sensitive and selective electrochemical biosensor was developed to recognize of miR-153 in human plasma samples. For the first time, the surface of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was decorated with poly-chitosan (CS) and silver nanoprisms/graphene quantum dots/ cysteamine A (AgNPrs/GQDs-Cys A) ink for the excellent immobilization of DNA towards sensitive and selective monitoring of micRNA-153. The designed novel electrochemical biosensor could detect miR-153 by differential pulse voltammetry with a linear range of 6.25 µM to 50 µM, which low limit of quantification is 6.25 µM. According to high selectivity, sensitivity, fast and easy operation this biodevice promising for clinical applications and high-throughput screening of PDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a common neurodegenerative illness among the elderly that can be caused by a variety of factors [1]. Over the nineteenth to twenty centuries, researchers have attempted to improve human knowledge of this most common progressive neurological disorder [1–4]. In the early stage of PD, dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra of the midbrain died or were disrupted [5,6]. Decreased rate of dopamine production due to destruction of neurons has caused various non-motor and motor side-effects PD such as rigidity, bradykinesia postural instability, tremor, and introduction PD as the world’s second most common neurodegenerative disease [7–9]. Thereby, a low concentration of dopamine is origination unusual brain activity of humans. PD is one of the main progressive nervous system sicknesses that reduces life span [4]. On the other hand, the sick symptoms were significantly limited by medications and surgical treatment [10]. Medications have acted directly and indirectly to the concentration of other brain chemicals and dopamine in the human body to relieve some symptoms. Variety of vital factors such as environmental triggers, gens, age (incidence rates close to 1 % in people over 60 years of age) also specific microscopic markers within brain cells have been impacted to stage of PD [11–13].

Up to now, scientists have been focused on early-stage diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases. However, there is still a massive shortage diagnosing of PDs toward pathogenesis of obscure and lack of objective laboratory detection methods [14]. Alpha-Synuclein (α-Synuclein) is a vital protein found within lewy bodies and associated with PD [15–17]. Over the last few years, investigation of α-Synuclein accumulation in the pathogenesis of PDs by genetic studies has established a central role [18]. miRNAs are non-coding and short RNA species with a post-transcriptional effect on miRNAs. These miRNAs have key roles in development, normal ageing and various disease such as PDs [19]. Among different miRNAs, MiR-153 has been recognized to regulate α-Synuclein expression levels post-transcriptionally in the brain [20]. Although overexpression of miR-153 significantly decreased endogenous α-Synuclein level, miR-153 inhibition increased translation of a luciferase construct bearing the α-Synuclein 3′-untranslated region in neurons. Therefore, the miR-153 role is to control fluctuations in endogenous α-synuclein protein level. It can also use the as high potential of PD biomarker for detection of this nervous system sickness.

To date various type of advanced nanomaterials and polymeric materials were used to electrochemical application [21–24]. Over the last decade, various advantages of electrochemical biosensors, including being practical, fast, selective, sensitive, and having high detection ability and low detection limits, have caused widespread applications of these types of biosensors in the field of sensing [25,26]. These biosensors have been carried out based on oxidation–reduction events upon interactions between their targets and bioreceptors on solid subtract of an electrode [27]. Recently, Cui and coworkers utilized fluorometric technique for label-free and ultra-sensitive detection of miR-153 [28]. They used the complex fluorescence bio-probe on the copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) by self-assembly technique. However, according to our best knowledge, there is no electrochemical biosensor for the detection of miR-153. In this study, a novel conductive ink was casted on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by poly-chitosan (P(CS)) to provide an appropriate DNA immobilization substrate. As an innovative research work, the surface of GCE was decorated with P(CS) and silver nanoprisms/graphene quantum dots/cysteamine A (AgNPrs/GQDs-Cys A) ink for the excellent immobilization of DNA towards sensitive and selective monitoring of micRNA-153 in real samples. Interestingly, miR-153 was detected by DNA-based electrochemical biosensor using hybridization regime. The modification surface of the developed electrochemical biosensor (GCE/poly-CS/ink/DNA) could efficiently determine miR-153 with LLOQ of the 6.25 µM. Obtained analytical results show that, developed biosensor has appropriated capability to use in clinical studies. It is important to point out that, this is the first report on application of conductive inks for the analysis of miR-153. So, there is various electrochemical opportunities for the development of this research area in near future.

All chemicals used in this research area in an analytical grade. Potassium ferrocyanide K4Fe(CN)6, potassium ferricyanide K3Fe(CN)6, citric acid, silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), mercaptoethanole (MCH), ethanol, CS and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada). Furthermore, Tris-HCl (hydroxymethyl), cysteine (Cys), were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). DNA (sequence, GAT CAC TTT TGT GAC TAT GCAA), miR-153 (sequence, UUG CAU AGU CAC AAA AGU GAUC), three mismatches (sequence, UAG CAU AGU CAG AAA AGU CAUC) and non-complement DNA with a sequence of GCC CGG AGA GCU GGG AGC CAG were purchase from Pishgam biotech (Tehran, Iran). Deionized water and human plasma samples were prepared from Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran) and Ghazi Pharmaceutical Company (Tabriz, Iran), respectively. 0.01 M KCl and 0.01 M of K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 (1:1) was used to ferricyanide/ferrocyanide as electrochemical assessment of the microscopic surface areas of the working electrodes.

AUTOLAB PGSTAT 302 N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) system and ordinary-three-electrode cell (from Metrohm) containing Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, a platinum wire as an auxiliary electrode and a GCE (from Azar Electrode Co., Urmia, Iran) as a working electrode (d = 2 mm) were used to electrochemical measurement and electrodeposition which was controlled by NOVA 1.1 software. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Adelaide, Australia, Philips CM30 electron microscope) and field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) were applied for studying the morphology of modification surfaces. Furthermore, EDX (model: MIRA3 TESCAN, Brno, Czech Republic) was used and analyzed the chemical compositions of the different preparation steps of the biosensor. DLS (Dynamic Light Scattering) with the zeta potential instrument Malvern Instruments ltd (Zetasizer Ver. 7.11, MAL1032660, England) were implemented to evaluation the size-frequency and zeta potential of the developed conductive ink. Also, the AFM (atomic force microscopy) images were recorded on a Bruker Dimension Icon microscope in the ScanAsyst regime with OTESPA AFM probes (Bruker).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized according to our previous work [29,30]. The acetone was then appended to the resulting GQDs to give a two-phase solution. The upper phase was discarded and the lower phase was lyophilized. 2 g of GQDs obtained from lyophilization was dissolved in 1: 1 ratio of water: dimethyl sulfoxide and then mixed with 8 g of Cys A. In order to activate and improve the binding of CysA to GQDs, 5 g of DCC (N, N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide) and 0.05 g of DMAP (4-(Dimethylamino)pyridine) were added to the above mixture and magnetically stirred at 60 °C. After 24 h, the resulting mixture was filtered and washed with distilled water and acetone. Finally, CysA-functionalized (GQDs-Cys A) were dissolved in a concentration of 1 mg/mL in 1 M sodium hydroxide solution and homogenized by ultrasonication and used.

Silver nanoprisms (AgNPrs) were produced under our prior works [31,32]. To synthesize of Ag NPrs/GQDs-Cys A conductive nano-ink, a composite was first made by dissolving graphite powder (0.6 g) and PVP (0.2 g) in CS solution. Then, GQDs-Cys A (2 mL) was added into the over mixture and stirred magnetically. Following, Ag NPrs (600 µL) was added and incubated at 60 °C for 12 h. Thereafter, the mixture was stirred at 80 °C and NaOH solution was added. Subsequently, the prepared solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min. Eventually, it was washed 3 times and dispersed into ethylene glycol (EG), ethanol and deionized water mixture with a volume ratio of 3:6:6 and sonicated for 30 min.

The electrode surface was cleaned by electrochemical methods and manual to remove any probable contaminant particle based on the cleaning protocol. For this purpose, the electrode surface was polished with 0.05 µm alumina (Beuhler, USA) on a standard pad and then the electrode was rinsed with a solution of deionized water for obtaining a mirror-like surface. Furthermore, in the other to further cleaning, GCE was included in the solution of H2SO4 (0.5 M) and cycled 20 times between −0.2 to 0.8 V by CV technique. After pre-treatment, the electrodes were dried at room temperature.

The preparation steps of the developed biosensor (GCE/poly-CS/ink/DNA/MCH) consist of electropolymerization of CS, modification GCE/poly-CS with synthesized ink and immobilization of specific DNA. First of all, the surface of the bare electrode was modified with CS (0.005 g, 0.1 M acetic acid) as high surface area scaffold materials for providing appropriate modification surface of Ag NPrs/GQDs-CysA ink by CV technique in the range of −0.6 to 0.2 V with scan rate of 50 mV/s (Figure S1 (see supporting information)). Afterwards, the surface of GCE/poly-CS was decorated with synthesized ink by its incubation at 7 h, temperature 50 °C.

Then, specific DNAs with a sequence of GAT CAC TTT TGT GAC TAT GCAA were immobilized on the GCE modified by poly-CS/ink for 12 h at the 4 °C temperature by drop casting technique. 10 µL MCH (as efficient blocker agent) was dropped on the surface of GCE/poly-CS/ink/DNA for blocking non-specific sites of the electrode (30 min, 25 °C). Finally, 10 µL of miR-154 was dropped on the surface of modified GCE for 30 min at 37 °C (Scheme 1 ).

In other to study the morphology and size of AgNPrs/GQDs/CysA ink, FE-SEM images of synthesized ink were recorded (Figure S2A-H(see supporting information)). As previously mentioned, AgNPrs and GQDs are arranged on the surface of graphite sheets. Furthermore, the size of these nanoparticles is between 202.41 and 295.29 nm. Elements in the nano-ink composition were also evaluated using the EDX method. EDX results confirmed the presence of graphite by presenting carbon. In addition, the element of silver is clearly visible (Figure S2I(see supporting information)). Therefore, results confirm the correct arrangement of the polymer sheets. In the following, the DLS results shown in Figure S3 (see supporting information) reveal that the particle size is around 1000 nm to 10000 nm with a zeta potential of − 6 mV (Figure S3(see supporting information)). DLS is a simple, reproducible, and inexpensive method for investigating particle size distribution. For studying ink samples at nanoscale, AFM as powerful and versatile microscopy technology was used at a frequency of 1 Hz (Figure S4(see supporting information)). The TEM images are shown in Figure S5 (see supporting information) reveal that the nanoparticles have a core–shell structure with a core of a shell of silver. Therefore, the results of all tests have proven that the ink was successfully synthesized.

As shown in Figure S6-S8(see supporting information), the FE-SEM imaging obtained results and EDX results for three modified GCE by poly-CS/AgNPrs/GQDs/CysA ink, poly-CS/AgNPrs/GQDs/CysA ink/DNA, poly-CS/ink/DNA MCH/miRNA-153, respectively. Poly-CS was utilized to adhere the ink to the GCE. As the images that revealed in Figure S6A-I (see supporting information) reveal, AgNPrs/GQDs/CysA ink was successfully immobilized on polymer films. The formation of ink shown in different magnifications in Figure S6A-I (see supporting information) with an average diameter of 67.91 nm has been recorded. Furthermore, EDX results confirmed the presents of N and O elements on the surface of GCE.

FE-SEM showed in Fig. 1 A-I was recorded after utilizing DNA as a bioreceptor with the lowest detected diameter of 87.69 nm. As well as, EDX obtained results reveals that in addition to P and N as prepared matrix elements, oxygen also appeared at the matrix as one of the elements in the DNA structure (Fig. 1J). Compared to the previous stage (poly-CS/ink), the structures containing the pores in the images are observed, which demonstrates the success of the electrode modification process at this stage.

The subsequent, stage of miRNA-153 incubation in different magnifications is shown in Figure S7A-I(see supporting information). As can be seen, the miRNA-153 successfully conjugated with modification surface. Based on the EDX curve, the results confirm the presence of elements N, O and P on the surface of the modified electrode (Figure S7J(see supporting information)).

To appraise the conductivity of synthesized conductive nano-ink, the conductive pattern was handly formed by AgNPrs/GQDs-Cys A nano-ink on the superficies of photographic paper. Cathode legs of LED (1.5 V) were connected to battery (3 V) anode and so the cathode surface of the battery was attached to one side of conductive lines. Then, the anode leg of the LED touched another side of conductive paths, the electrical circuit switched on and the LED was lighted up. Resistance was also appraised by an ohmmeter. The flexibility of conductive paths was evaluated by bending it 90˚. The various conductive routes, dip LED and battery formed on the substrate is displayed in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evolution of biosensor fabricated steps
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigation of the electrochemical performance in each step of fabrication, DPV as the powerful voltammetric technique was operated in supporting electrolyte solution containing a 1:1 ratio of ferrocyanide/ferricyanide with 0.5 M KCl (as electrochemical probe) at physiological pH (pH = 7.4) in a potential range of −1 to + 1 V. The obtained results demonstrated that decorated GCE surface with thickness layer of poly-CS has led to slightly decrement of the current peak from 3.03 to 2.27 µA. Although the presence of this polymer has caused to decrease rate of electron transfer, several functional group and large surface area can introduce appropriate substrate for binding with ink due to a large number of functional groups in the surface of this bioactive polymer including amine, methyl and hydroxyl group. As shown in Fig. 3 , the modification surface of GCE with AgNPrs/GQDs-CysA ink has led to an insignificant increase in current peak. The presence of AgNPrs in the structure of developed ink has increased the conductivity of modified surface due to the high ability of metallic materials in electron transfer. The impressive shift in peak current from 2.57 to 3.75 µA revealed successful immobilization of DNA as a specific bioreceptor on the engineered substrate by a covalent bond (Fig. 3). Furthermore, enhancing the peak current after incubation of electrode in MCH as blocking agent which could successfully inactive free functional groups of developed ink. Finally, because of the conjugation of miR-153 with specific DNA on the surface of the modified electrode, the peak current decreased to 13.1 µA, which proves that the developed biosensor is an electrochemical “Signal ON” biosensing platform for miR-153 detection.

The incubation time of miR-153 required DNA-miRNA-153 is an important parameter that determines the performance of the biosensor. In the other to optimize the time required for the complete bio-reaction to take place, 10 µL of miR-153 was incubated on the modified surface of the electrode for various periods times including 5, 10, 15, 20, and 30 min. DPV technique was operated in the presence of [Fe(CN)6]3− /4− solution to determine the optimized time of incubation using measurement of peak current and potential versus different period times. As shown in Figure (S8A-C(see supporting information)), a time of 30 min exhibited a stable response indicating the complete interaction between DNA and miR-153.

DPV technique as a sensitive electrochemical technique was exploited for detection of the different concentrations of miR-153 (50, 25, 12.5, and 6.25 µM) by using of GCE/poly(CS)/ink/DNA in an electrochemical cell containing 0.01 M ferrocyanide/ferricyanide solution at physiological pH (7.4) at room temperature (Fig. 4 ). In the presence of the various concentration of miR-153, DPVs response was recorded in a potential range of −1 V to + 1 V and scan rate of 100 mV/s. Under the optimal experimental conditions, a good direct relationship between the various concentration of miR-153 and current of the peak was observed. As presented in the DPVs, with the enhancement of miR-153 concentration, the peak current increases as shown in Fig. 4B. Thereby, the fabricated biosensor was operated based on a signal on the system. The linear range and LLOQ of the developed biosensor were 6.25 to 50 µM and 6.25 µM, respectively. In addition, the linear regressions equation obtained from DPV in the presence of different concentration of miR-153 was:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of developed biosensor in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In the other to evaluate the effect of different concentrations of miR-153 in human plasma samples as in real sample, miR-153 was spiked into human plasma with the ratio of 1:1 (v/v) and incubated on the surface of the engineered electrode (GCE/poly(CS)/ink/DNA). Figure S9 (see supporting information) demonstrated the DPVs plot of genosensor in the presence of various concentrations of miR-153 (25, 12.5 and 6.25 µM) spiked in human plasma samples. The intensity of the peak current decreases by reducing the hybridization of the miR-153 with DNA complex. Furthermore, the linear regression equation is as follow:

In the other to demonstrate the ability of the bioreceptor to detect a particular analyte in the presence of an interfering agent target, selectivity is a significant feature. Thus, the ability to accurately detect the miR-153 biomarker was evaluated by DPV technique in the presence of two other sequences including three mismatch and non-complement DNA (nc-DNA). As shown in Fig. 5 , three mismatch and non-complement DNA have not interfered in the detection of miR-153. In detail, the current peak of miR-153, three mismatch and non-complement DNA were 8.74, 6.10 and 2.44 µA, respectively.

Repeatability was described as the ability of the biosensor to generate identical responses for a duplicated experimental set-up. This important factor is determined by the accuracy of the converter and electronics. To investigate the reproducibility, the DPVs were recorded for four separate electrodes that were prepared by the same protocol. The resulting flows were not significantly different. Therefore, it can be concluded that the developed biodevice can be reproduced (Figure S10 (see supporting information)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term and short-term stability of fabricated ink and biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Stability is vital feature for point-of-care diagnostic applications of biosensors. In the present work, the fabricated biosensor was stored at 4 °C during the storage time. The stability of the fabricated biosensor and ink were monitored by the CV technique. Based to obtained results, the developed genosensor has acceptable stability which is related to the high stability of designed ink and the use of poly(CS) for chemical binding of inks to GCE. As can see in Figure S11A-D(see supporting information), the intraday and inter-day stability of ink on the modified surface (GCE/poly(CS)) were increased till the second day and 6 h, respectively. In intraday stability study, the CVs of the ink on GCE/poly(CS) was recorded every 24 h. As shown in Figure S11B(see supporting information), the electron transfer on the GCE/poly(CS)/ink was 523 % increased and as well as inter-day test demonstrated approximately 235 % enhancing current of peak after 6 h (Figure S11D(see supporting information)).

The obtained result of stability was revealed a similar result. In detail, the peak current of CVs in intraday and inter-day were approximately increased 138 and 100 % till second day and 4 h, respectively. Overall, all stability tests prove that the fabricated biosensor has the optimum performance until the second day and as time passes, its sensitivity falls. It is important to point out that, low stability of the conductive ink is major limitation of the present work. We are trying to overcome in this limitation by synthesis of novel conductive inks with high stability.

Studies of electrochemical kinetic are important for the investigation of oxidation reactions in organic molecules. For this purpose, redox behavior of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−/KCl using GCE/P(CS)-ink was investigated by CV technique in the potential range of −1 to + 1 V at the various sweep rates (10–700 mV/s). According to Figure S12A(see supporting information), the peak current and oxidation peak potential have a direct relationship with sweep rate. The acquired related plot was demonstrated in Figure S12B(see supporting information), which demonstrated the electrochemical activity of surface redox couples.

Ip =
n
2
F
2
4 R T
ν A Γ
∗
(1)where potential sweep rate (ν), electrode surface area (A = πr2 = 0.0314 cm2), R-universal gas constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1), F-Faraday constant (96,487C mol−1) and T-Kelvin temperature (298 K). Г* has been obtained as 3.2971 mol.cm −2 by equation (1). Furthermore, variation of peak current versus square root of demonstrated in Figure S12C (see supporting information) which linear slope associated to it, reliable to indicate mass transfer controlling the process of oxidation occurred via diffusion. Furthermore, the obtained result of Figure S12F (see supporting information) was conformed diffusion control of the redox process (slop = 0.56).

In summary, the GCE/poly(CS)/AgNPrs-GQDs-CysA nano-ink/DNA was exploited as an efficient detection substrate of miR-153. In this developed biosensor, a large number of functionalized groups on the poly(CS) have caused the creation appropriate matrix for the stabilized casting of synthesized conductive ink. Furthermore, the high surface area and high conductivity of ink could provide an excellent modification surface for the immobilization of unique DNA for capturing miR-153. Under the optimized operating conditions, the fabricated biosensor revealed an acceptable linear range of 6.25 to 50 µM and LLOQ of 6.25 µM for the determination of miR-153, respectively. All things considered, the application of biosensors in the biomedical field is in the early-stage and it is expected more developed in utilizing the proposed biosensor as a versatile system for other biomarkers due to potential in biological and clinical diagnoses.
Availability of data and materials.
Data sharing is not applicable. It will be shared if needed.

Esmaeil Darvish Aminabad: Writing – original draft. Mohammad Hasanzadeh: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Arezoo Saadati: Writing – original draft. Mohammad Ali Hosseinpour Feizi: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Reza Safaralizadeh: Writing – review & editing. Ahmad Mobed: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: [All the authors (E. Darvish Aminabad, M. Hasanzadeh, A. Saadati, M. Ali Hosseinpour Feizi, R. Safaralizadeh, and A. Mobed) have read, approved and made substantial contributions for the manuscript. None of the original material contained in this manuscript has been previously published nor is currently under review for publication elsewhere. Besides, authors declare no conflict of interests and/or commercial products or companies.].‬‬‬‬‬‬

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the University of Tabriz and Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2022.116017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An essay on the shaking palsy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Parkinson, An essay on the shaking palsy, The Journal of neuropsychiatry and clinical neurosciences, 14 (2002) 223-236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new look at James Parkinson's Essay on the Shaking Palsy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.A. Kempster, B. Hurwitz, A.J. Lees, A new look at James Parkinson's Essay on the Shaking Palsy, Neurology, 69 (2007) 482-485.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine neuron systems in the brain: an update
